EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow developed within the warm sector ahead of a potent cold front. Wind gusts reached as high as 40-50 mph across parts of the area due to strong gradient winds well in advance of the cold front. A line of low topped convection with little to no lightning entered western portions of the forecast area shortly after 8 pm and moved off the coast by midnight, quickly followed by the cold front itself. Strong dynamical forcing accompanied the front, but surface based instability was near zero most of the evening. The low topped convection was supported by 0-6 km shear values of 60-80 kt along with favorable upper level divergence from a 150 kt jet. Mid level lapse rates were poor throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bulloch County 911 Center reported at least 3 trees down in Statesboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure dominated the central portion of the country with a weak shortwave over Georgia, bringing a light rain/snow mix to north Georgia early in the morning.  Otherwise, cool and dry conditions prevailed, though relative humidity values were above critical levels for dangerous fire weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Jasus burned 0.1 acres and did not result in any damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 24th, a warm front was situated along the Georgia/Florida border, with an area of surface low pressure along the Florida Panhandle.  The surface low worked its way slowly east and northeast along the warm front as it lifted north into central Georgia during the morning hours.  A shortwave aloft pushed through the Southeast, helping provide lift for the convection that developed.  Several thunderstorms developed during the morning hours; some reached severe levels with mainly hail and some tree damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Employees at the Little Ocmulgee State Park reported that a few trees were blown down on the golf course.  A porch also had its roof blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed surface low moved through the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys on the 5th.  This pushed a cold front across Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours.  A squall line ahead of the front state, with damage occurring in northwest Georgia in the early afternoon and continuing into central Georgia through the mid-evening hours.  Widespread wind damage, mainly in the form of fallen trees, occurred, but one tornado was observed as well in Glascock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Murray County 911 Center reported that numerous trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak upper level ridging persisted across the area on the 17th.  A shortwave had moved through the area the previous day, and the remnants of a mesoscale convective system (MCS) that moved through West Virginia as a result had worked its way south-southwest into eastern Tennessee and western North Carolina during the day on the 17th.  With surface flow to the southwest and flow aloft to the northeast, the storms that developed along the outflow from the remnant MCS moved very little.  Thunderstorms that developed in the north Georgia mountains during the late afternoon and evening remained nearly stationary, resulting in flash flooding in Fannin and Union Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilscot Creek flooded around six different roads with up to two feet of water and damaged two county bridges and three private bridges. Portions of Old Dial Road were affected the most with a camper being washed away and getting crushed under a bridge. Rapid flowing flood waters caused a propane tank to break away from a house and float downstream. The flooding also surrounded and entered a house which temporarily stranded an elderly couple. A land slide occurred on a portion of Daves Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly dry pattern was in place on the 20th; a few showers moved through the area early in the day but surface high pressure built into the area during the afternoon.  Relative humidity values did approach critical levels during the afternoon, but fuel moistures remained too high for fire danger conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a wildfire of unknown cause labeled Shooting Range burned 0.1 acres and resulted in $300 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cool wedge of surface high pressure ridging down the eastern slopes of the Appalachians dominated the area on the 22nd.  With cloud cover in place, relative humidity values remained well above critical thresholds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Callihan Road burned 0.25 acres and resulted in $1,500 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse frontal boundary lingered just north of the Altamaha River basin. with light SSE steering flow over the area. Precipitable water content was high around 2 inches. Light steering flow, high  moisture content, and low level forcing from the front and sea breezes produced locally heavy, flooding rainfall in Jesup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 9:47 pm EDT, Wayne County Dispatch reported that a portion of Odum Screven Road was flooded near Route 203. By 10:22 pm EDT, Dispatch reported that many roads in Jesup were flooded, and a few structures had water flowing into them. By 10:45 pm, the county emergency manager relayed that there was widespread flash flooding occurring in Jesup. Numerous roads were flooded including Main Road 84. There were 4 vehicles completely covered in water, and water was entering homes, motels and fire stations in Jesup. Around 12 am on 8/20, the emergency manager relayed that 20 to 30 homes in downtown Jesup were flooded with about 1 ft of standing water. There was about 4 inches of standing water at the main fire station in Jesup. A motel in Jesup was also flooded. U. S. Highway 84 was closed due to flooding Monday evening, but was re-opened over night. Wayne county closed public schools on Tuesday, 8/20, due to flooded roads. The cost of damage was unknown, but was estimated for the event to be included in Storm Data. The estimate is likely grossly underestimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse frontal boundary lingered just north of the Altamaha River basin. with light SSE steering flow over the area. Precipitable water content was high around 2 inches. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous secondary county roads remained covered by flood waters from recent heavy rainfall. Appling county schools were closed on Tuesday, 8/20, due to flood waters. The cost of damage was unknown, but it was estimated such that this flood event could be included in StormData. The estimate is likely grossly underestimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed surface low moved through the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys on the 5th.  This pushed a cold front across Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours.  A squall line ahead of the front state, with damage occurring in northwest Georgia in the early afternoon and continuing into central Georgia through the mid-evening hours.  Widespread wind damage, mainly in the form of fallen trees, occurred, but one tornado was observed as well in Glascock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Haralson County 911 Center reported that several trees had blown down east of Buchanan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong storm system that brought severe weather to the area on March 5th moved to the east overnight, with much colder air filtering in behind the exiting cold front.  As the upper level low deepened across the southern Appalachians on the morning of the 6th, wraparound moisture filtered into north Georgia.  This moisture combined with the northwest flow and cold temps allowed precipitation to fall as snow in the mountains beginning early on the 6th, accumulating to as much as 3 to 4 inches in the highest elevations of the northeast mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County Emergency Managers, employees of the U.S. Forest Service, and employees of the various Georgia State Parks across the northeast mountains (including Vogel State Park in Union County, Amicalola Falls State Park in Dawson County, Fort Mountain State Park in Murray County, Smithgall Woods Sate Park) all reported between 2 to 4 inches of snowfall.  Most of the snow fell at the highest elevations, generally above 2500 feet.  Snowfall accumulations at lower elevations, where higher populations are concentrated, generally totaled less than 2 inches, but because of strong winds and blowing snow leading to reduced visibilities, several traffic accidents were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Three inches of rainfall were measured in 45 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several days of heavy rain, flash flooding developed quickly over parts of northeast Georgia as another round of heavy rain moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous roads were closed due to flooding in the Cornelia, Baldwin, Alto and Mt Airy communities. Streams reportedly threatened homes, though there were no reports of significant damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper ridge over Florida helped to suppress convection across the Southeast, but afternoon instability still allowed a few showers and thunderstorms to develop especially across north Georgia.  Fuel moistures and relative humidity values were above critical levels, but one wildfire did occur.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Mack White burned 1.94 acres and resulted in $1500 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow continued in place aloft, with a strong upper high over the Southern Plains and a deep trough over the Eastern Seaboard.  A weak surface boundary remained stalled across central Georgia on the 5th, helping to trigger showers and thunderstorms.  One thunderstorm became severe with large hail reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter sized hail between Nunez and Oak Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level disturbance moved through the area during the afternoon.  With a warm and moist airmass in place, several showers and thunderstorms developed.  One brought down a tree and resulted in a lightning fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County Emergency Manager reported that a business was struck by lightning and burned for a total loss.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public reported trees down along Gay Road between William Tinley Road and Hwy 305.  Also, numerous limbs were down and pea size hail occurred.

Behind this rain, a wake low developed and moved across central Georgia, bringing wind gusts between 35 and 45 MPH.  Because of the saturated soils, a few trees came down with these strong winds.

One of these storms became severe with trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Catoosa County 911 Center reported several trees down, including along Highway 41, Salem Valley Road, and Dogwood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very dry northwest flow was in place on the 21st.  The combination of drying fuels, low relative humidity values and northwest winds between 10 and 15 MPH led to dangerous fire weather conditions across a large portion of the area, especially across north and east central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Brittany burned 0.1 acres and resulted in $500 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through the Southeast on the 19th.  Instability increased ahead of the front across central Georgia during the afternoon hours, and several thunderstorms were able to become severe, with mostly damaging winds reported.  A tornado occurred as well in Newton and Morgan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A  National Weather Service storm survey determined that an EF-2 tornado formed within a line of storms moving through Newton County.  It first touched down along Dukes Road, southwest of Mansfield, where it snapped and uprooted a few trees.  As the tornado moved east along Kellogg Street it increased in intensity, producing EF-1 damage to several homes along Kellogg Street and Savannah Street.  The EF-2 damage occurred to an older home on the north side of Kellogg Street where the home was completely destroyed.  A mobile home further east along Kellogg Street was also completely destroyed.  One person was injured in the mobile home and was trapped within the debris.  Numerous trees and power lines were snapped and downed along Kellogg Stret as well as further east along Sewell and Lloyd Roads.  In all, eight homes sustained significant damage and a few buildings in downtown Mainsfield sustained minor damage.  The tornado began to weaken as it headed northeast crossing CR-213 where a park sustaned damage to several outbuildings and several trees were uprooted.  Minor tree damage was noted in the town of Newborn as the tornado moved through the north side of town.  The tornado then crossed into Morgan County.|[04/19/13: Tornado #1, County #1-2, EF2, Newton-Morgan, 2013:008].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through the Southeast on the 19th.  Instability increased ahead of the front across central Georgia during the afternoon hours, and several thunderstorms were able to become severe, with mostly damaging winds reported.  A tornado occurred as well in Newton and Morgan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that touched down in Newton County crossed into Morgan County briefly.  The track ended near the intersection of Durden Road and Reese Road where a few trees were snapped and uprooted.|[04/19/13: Tornado #1, County #2-2, EF2, Newton-Morgan, 2013:008].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With strong high pressure positioned to the northeast, a wedge of high pressure was in place east of the Appalachians. A tight northeast pressure gradient was present along the Georgia coast which produced gusty winds, knocking down a few trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on a car at Waters Avenue and Johnston Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across portions of southeast Georgia under the influence of weak high pressure and significant surface heating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported ten minutes of dime to nickel size hail in Guyton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Screven County communications center reported that lightning struck a tree which then fell on a car in downtown Sylvania.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reports hail up to the size of quarters on Flowers Road near highway 196.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: SW steering flow combined with high moisture content and passing upper level energy fueled scattered afternoon thunderstorms which produced gusty winds and a tornado ENE of Bostwick.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four trees were blown down along Sardis Church Road. The time of the event was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 24th, a warm front was situated along the Georgia/Florida border, with an area of surface low pressure along the Florida Panhandle.  The surface low worked its way slowly east and northeast along the warm front as it lifted north into central Georgia during the morning hours.  A shortwave aloft pushed through the Southeast, helping provide lift for the convection that developed.  Several thunderstorms developed during the morning hours; some reached severe levels with mainly hail and some tree damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Parishioners at a local church and an employee of the Flat Creek Lodge reported several trees down on the east side of Summertown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed surface low moved through the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys on the 5th.  This pushed a cold front across Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours.  A squall line ahead of the front state, with damage occurring in northwest Georgia in the early afternoon and continuing into central Georgia through the mid-evening hours.  Widespread wind damage, mainly in the form of fallen trees, occurred, but one tornado was observed as well in Glascock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Catoosa County 911 Center reported several trees down in the north end of the county, including one at Beaver Avenue and another at Lafayette Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed through the Southeast on the 19th. A tornado occurred as well in Newton and Morgan Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County Emergency Manager reported that a tree was blown down across Highway 48 near Ridge Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the area at the end of January with unusually strong wind fields.  The 500 mb winds were as high as 110 knots with the upper level trough, and 850 mb winds reached around 70 knots in the local area. These very strong low level winds combined with SBCAPE values of 300-500 j/kg produced widespread straight line wind damage across the area with numerous trees and power lines blown down as a squall line moved through the area.  Strong non-convective related winds also occurred ahead of the main line of convection and resulted in a few trees being blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree and power line were blown down across Highway 97 near Hopeful.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge remained over the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes area, with easterly flow dominating across the Southeast.  An upper disturbance moving across in the easterly flow combined with the continued moist and unstable airmass allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many of these became severe with damaging winds and even some reports of large hail.  Isolated flash flooding was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter to golf ball sized hail south of Palmetto.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low was in place across the Great Lakes, allowing continued intermittent upper level disturbances to rotate around and affect the Southeast.  A complicated surface pattern in place with multiple boundaries left over from previous days' convection as well as a weak nearly stationary front, combined with ample moisture and high levels of instability provided plenty of focus for thunderstorm development.  Numerous storms did indeed develop, and many of these became severe with mainly damaging winds reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High pressure off the Southeast coast continued to bring deep moisture into the area.  A weak area of low pressure over the Mid-Atlantic allowed disturbances to continue moving across the state, with several showers and thunderstorms developing in the afternoon.  Lightning associated with one of these storms resulted in two house fires in Henry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Henry County Emergency Manager reported that lightning started two house fires.  The fires were quickly extinguished with little damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into northwest Georgia during the morning hours of the 12th.  Very little precipitation was associated with the cold front, but winds increased behind the front to between 15 and 20 MPH and gusting to over 25 MPH, creating high fire danger conditions.  Dry high pressure moved in behind the front, with low relative humidity values creating high fire danger conditions through the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Stateline burned 6.7 acres and resulted in $6200 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front lifted northward over south Georgia with weaker SW steering flow in place. Diurnal heating combined with sea breeze and outflow activity spawned a few severe storms over the area during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large pine tree was blown down onto a home which caused extensive damage. The cost of damage was unknown. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface front lifted northward over south Georgia with weaker SW steering flow in place. Diurnal heating combined with sea breeze and outflow activity spawned a few severe storms over the area during the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County dispatch officials reported a tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A diffuse frontal boundary lingered just north of the Altamaha River basin. with light SSE steering flow over the area. Precipitable water content was high around 2 inches. Light steering flow, high  moisture content, and low level forcing from the front and sea breezes produced locally heavy, flooding rainfall in Jesup.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: U.S. Highway 1 at mile marker 6 near Alma was inundated by flood waters from recent heavy rainfall. The cost of damage was unknown but was estimated for the event to be included in StormData. It is likely grossly underestimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low across the northern Gulf of Mexico allowed increasing moisture to filter into the Southeast on the 20th.  A strong shortwave moved out of the Gulf during the morning hours and across the Southeast during the afternoon.  Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain developed again, leading to isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported a small creek rapidly rising and out of its banks behind homes on Jewel Court in the Guilford Forest area. Minor flooding occurred in the residential backyards.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low across the northern Gulf of Mexico allowed increasing moisture to filter into the Southeast on the 20th.  A strong shortwave moved out of the Gulf during the morning hours and across the Southeast during the afternoon.  Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain developed again, leading to isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height of a USGS stream gage on the Nickajack Creek at Veterans Memorial Highway near Mableton reached flood stage of 10 feet. The creek crest at 10.3 feet. Minor flooding occurred in portions of Nickajack Park upstream from the gage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low was in place across the Great Lakes, allowing continued intermittent upper level disturbances to rotate around and affect the Southeast.  A complicated surface pattern in place with multiple boundaries left over from previous days' convection as well as a weak nearly stationary front, combined with ample moisture and high levels of instability provided plenty of focus for thunderstorm development.  Numerous storms did indeed develop, and many of these became severe with mainly damaging winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported half dollar sized hail to the north-northeast of Cochran.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very similar conditions were in place  on the 25th as on the 24th, with ample Gulf and Atlantic moisture in place across the Southeast and another upper level disturbance moving across the area.  Once again, several showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon, and a couple of these were strong.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Toombs County 911 Center reported that two trees were blown down near the intersection of Turner Bridge Road and Buddy Mosley Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A complex of thunderstorms over the Tennessee Valley weakened and moved into north Georgia on the morning of the 27th.  This complex produced an outflow boundary, which provided a focus for thunderstorm development during the afternoon, especially with the combination of continued ample moisture in place as well as increasing instability with the heating of the day.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off-duty National Weather Service employee reported pea to penny sized hail along Highway 74 near the golf cart bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave moved from the Ohio Valley into the Mid Atlantic States during the day.  A surface cold front pushed into north Georgia ahead of the shortwave during the morning, progressing into central Georgia during the afternoon hours.  Temperatures climbed into the 90s across most of the area with strong instability in place as well as deep layer shear, supportive of severe thunderstorm development.  Numerous severe thunderstorms did indeed develop with mainly downed trees and some large hail, but two small tornadoes also touched down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gordon County 911 Center reported numerous trees and power lines down across the county, including a few along Highway 61.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved south into the region and stalled, a series of upper level disturbances moved through the area with broad southwest flow and moist conditions.  As the boundary became oriented parallel to the upper level winds, training bands of convection set up, particularly across southwest Georgia where flash flooding and areal flooding occurred.  Radar estimated that rainfall amounts of 8-10 inches occurred in a swath from Early county northeastward to Ben Hill county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were closed due to flash flooding north of Colquitt around U.S Highway 27 and State Highway 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front pushed into northwest Georgia during the morning hours of the 12th.  Very little precipitation was associated with the cold front, but winds increased behind the front to between 15 and 20 MPH and gusting to over 25 MPH, creating high fire danger conditions.  Dry high pressure moved in behind the front, with low relative humidity values creating high fire danger conditions through the 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Charlie's Creek burned 3.1 acres and resulted in $3300 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid-to-upper level shortwave swept across the Southern Appalachians early on the 2nd, with scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms developing early in the morning. During the day, the remnants of a squall line over the Lower Mississippi Valley pushed into the Southeast.  This combined with the shortwave and daytime heating allowed scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop.  Some thunderstorms reached severe levels with mainly damaging winds, but there were a couple of hail reports as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Peach County Emergency Manager reported dime to penny sized hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Long County emergency manager confirmed damage to a boat house and several trees snapped onto power lines on Gordon Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the area at the end of January with unusually strong wind fields.  The 500 mb winds were as high as 110 knots with the upper level trough, and 850 mb winds reached around 70 knots in the local area. These very strong low level winds combined with SBCAPE values of 300-500 j/kg produced widespread straight line wind damage across the area with numerous trees and power lines blown down as a squall line moved through the area.  Strong non-convective related winds also occurred ahead of the main line of convection and resulted in a few trees being blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down across Highway 32 between I-75 and the Irwin-Turner county line.  This was reported by the public via the WALB-TV facebook page.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southwest flow aloft was in place across the Southeast as a closed upper low pushed across the south central states during the day.  A cold front approached Georgia from the west on the evening of the 21st.  However, although relative humidity values and cloud cover increased ahead of the cold front, a wildfire was able to start burning in Union County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Deavers Road burned 1.23 acres and resulted in $450 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the area at the end of January with unusually strong wind fields.  The 500 mb winds were as high as 110 knots with the upper level trough, and 850 mb winds reached around 70 knots in the local area. These very strong low level winds combined with SBCAPE values of 300-500 j/kg produced widespread straight line wind damage across the area with numerous trees and power lines blown down as a squall line moved through the area.  Strong non-convective related winds also occurred ahead of the main line of convection and resulted in a few trees being blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of trees were blown down near Coleman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low was in place across the Great Lakes, allowing continued intermittent upper level disturbances to rotate around and affect the Southeast.  A complicated surface pattern in place with multiple boundaries left over from previous days' convection as well as a weak nearly stationary front, combined with ample moisture and high levels of instability provided plenty of focus for thunderstorm development.  Numerous storms did indeed develop, and many of these became severe with mainly damaging winds reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Fire Department dispatch reported that several trees and power lines were blown down across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tragic accident occurred when a tree was blown down onto a car. Both passengers were fatally injured.  Winds were sustained between 20-25 mph with gusts to 35 mph. A lake wind advisory was in effect.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very strong low level jet of 60-70 kts around 850 mb preceded a squall line as it moved over the forecast area. Instability was negligible, with one a few lightning strikes noted over western counties of SE Ga. Some of the strongest winds and minor wind damage occurred just ahead of the main squall line, which downed some trees and powerlines. Several clusters of convection became severe and produced more widespread tree damage...confined to the Suwannee River Valley and over SE Ga.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Stephen Foster State Park. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over NE Florida during the late afternoon over an unstable and moist airmass where the sea breezes and outflows converged. During the evening, and upper level disturbance over SE Georgia fueled severe storms where outflows converged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down onto a home and car. A tree was also blown down over the road near Raybon Road and West and Oak Grove.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over NE Florida during the late afternoon over an unstable and moist airmass where the sea breezes and outflows converged. During the evening, and upper level disturbance over SE Georgia fueled severe storms where outflows converged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on the road near the intersection of County Roads 125 and 127. The time of the damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: D2-D3 drought conditions continued through January and into February across a large portion of southwest Georgia.
D2-D3 drought conditions continued through January and into February across a large portion of southwest Georgia.

A short wave mid level trough was lingering over the southeast region. A piece of energy traveled over the area during peak heating and this instability and forcing combined with adequate mean layer moisture and forcing from the sea breezes spawned scattered afternoon and evening severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pea to penny size hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level cold pool aloft enhanced diurnal instability over a moist airmass. These ingredient fueled a couple of severe storms that initiated along the sea breezes and outflows during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Douglas. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A upper level cold pool aloft enhanced diurnal instability over a moist airmass. These ingredient fueled a couple of severe storms that initiated along the sea breezes and outflows during the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down in Willacoochee. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low across the southern Plains began bringing significant moisture into the state on the 28th, with widespread rain developing through the morning hours and heavy rain developing during the afternoon.  Despite these moistu conditions however, a wildfire was sparked during the early morning hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Boling Knob burned 7.31 acres and resulted in $800 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry high pressure was in place across the area, and a few locations especially in northwest Georgia were able to briefly experience critically low relative humidity values during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Hwy 286 burned 3.4 acres and resulted in $1000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread rain moved across the area the morning of the 10th as a series of shortwaves pushed through the Southeast.  Rain began moving out during the early afternoon hours, with dry high pressure building in thereafter.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Dry Creek burned 0.1 acres and resulted in $3000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry high pressure dominated the area on the 11th.  Relative humidity values dropped to the low to mid 20 percent range during the afternoon hours, creating high fire danger conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Little Amy Creek burned 2.5 acres and resulted in $7000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed across portions of Southeast Georgia in the evening hours along a westward moving cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Effingham County communications reports one large pine tree blown down along Long Acre Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Breezy and dry conditions were across the area on the 25th as high pressure dominated the Southeast.  While winds were gusty and relative humidity were low, fuel moistures were too high for high fire danger conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled FLT burned 0.1 acres and resulted in $200 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough axis pushed through the Southeast on the afternoon of the 16th, bringing some precipitation during the afternoon across mainly central Georgia.  Behind the trough axis, a dry airmass began to spread over the area.  However, because of significant recent rainfall, fuel moistures remained too high for any critical fire weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Hidden Creek burned 6.8 acres and resulted in $5,000 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front pushed through the area on the 1st, bringing a cold airmass to north and central Georgia.  High pressure built in behind the front, and pushed offshore on Saturday the 2nd.  An upper level clipper disturbance pushed through the Ohio Valley on the 2nd as well.  Meanwhile, two weak surface boundaries moved through the area, one on Saturday afternoon and the other Saturday night, both of which brought shallow moisture to the area.  With northwest winds, uplift as a result of topography enhanced the clipper system, and the resulting precipitation changed over to snow across portions of north Georgia.  In the end, snowfall accumulation totaled generally betwee 3 to 5 inches, mainly across the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gilmer County Emergency Manager reported that the snow began falling in the late morning and had accumulated to between 4 and 5 inches in the higher elevations by 630 PM.  In the lower elevations, accumulations were generally between 2 and 3 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers developed around midnight over the Blue Ridge counties of northeast Georgia. Snow showers continued through the early morning hours of the 6th before tapering off by mid-morning or so. Total accumulations ranged from less than an inch to around 2 inches.

Light sleet developed across much of the western Carolinas and northeast Georgia during the morning. The intermittent sleet eventually changed to light freezing rain in most areas by late afternoon. Most areas north of the I-85 corridor saw measurable sleet, generally less than a quarter inch. A light glaze then fell on top of that, making for treacherous driving conditions during the afternoon. Most areas south of the I-85 corridor saw only trace accumulations, but that was enough to cause plenty of slick spots. Hundreds of accidents were reported across the area, especially along the I-85 corridor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow showers moved away from the usual Tennessee border areas during the early morning hours of the 2nd, tapering off by late morning. Most locations near the southern Blue Ridge saw an inch or two of snow.
A stalled frontal boundary offshore combined with strong high pressure over the region produced strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast Media reported power outages due to numerous power lines down.

Precipitation developed across the mountains during the early morning hours. Precipitation fell mainly as snow in the high elevations, but snow levels quickly worked their way down to the valleys by sunrise before coming to an end. Most areas above 3000 feet saw 1 to 3 inches of snow. Valley locations saw accumulations from a light dusting to around an inch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbance rotating around a mean longwave trough west of the area brought copious amounts of moisture into the area.  The KTAE rawinsonde sounding on the evening of February 22nd measured a PWAT value of 1.77 inches, which was the 2nd highest PWAT observed during the month of February at that location.  Rainfall amounts from near 5 inches to excess of 13 inches were observed during the 5 day period between Feb 22-26.  This led to areas of flooding.  A couple of severe storms also occurred during the early morning hours of Feb 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rainfall over a long duration led to a gradual accumulation of water that caused areal flooding.  Around 9 roads were closed, some of which were heavily damaged.  Water reached the bottom of the bridge at Old Brookfield Road from Christian Way to Carpenter Road.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported that Pierce Chapel Road was flooded, and the yards of several homes in the area were flooded with driveways blocked.  This was mainly due to the persistent moderate to heavy rainfall over a long duration and saturated ground.  Around 12.2 inches of rain fell over a 3-4 day period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms moved through the CSRA producing some large hail and damaging winds.  A tornado also developed causing considerable damage to several farms in rural Burke County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF1 Tonado touched down about 12 miles southwest of Waynesboro taking down trees, severely damaging field irrigation systems and destroying cattle barns and heavy equipment barns at several locations.  A few cattle were killed and others injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal squall line of storms moved across southeast Georgia and northeast Florida through the day. Strong low level jet winds combined with sfc based instability and dry air entrainment supported a few severe storms over the area during the morning hours. By the afternoon as the line pushed southeast of northeast Florida...drier air limited convective formation and the severe storm threat greatly decreased.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were blown down in Travisville, located between Homerville and Argyle. The time of damage was based on radar imagery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Surface high pressure moved into the area on the 14th, with a cold front approaching the state.  Dry weather prevailed, but relative humidity values remained above fire danger criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a human-caused wildfire labeled Washrider 2 burned 0.12 acres and resulted in $500 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light snow developed over the Georgia mountains around noon, and continued through the afternoon. Snow, heavy at times, continued through early evening. Total accumulations generally ranged from 2 to 4 inches across much of Rabun County. While the continuous snowfall ceased during mid-evening, occasional snow showers persisted into the morning hours of the 3rd. However, additional accumulation was spotty.
A couple of isolated severe thunderstorms produced some large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sheriff reported nickel to quarter size hail in the Thomson area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked up from the Gulf of Mexico triggering a few thunderstorms in southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree limbs  were reported down across the area. The size of limbs were estimated at least 4 inches in diameter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked up from the Gulf of Mexico triggering a few thunderstorms in southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were reported down across the southwestern portion of Sapelo Island. One tree fell on a parked car. Damage to numerous aluminum signs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system tracked up from the Gulf of Mexico triggering a few thunderstorms in southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 86 MPH wind gust was measured at the National Estuarine Research system at Sapelo Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached from the west, supported by a potent shortwave. Temperatures in the mid 80s helped increase instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Law Enforcement reported 2 trees down along Highway 17 North.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted northward over the Florida peninsula. During the afternoon, the front combined with an upper level short wave trough combined to trigger scattered severe storms over southeast Georgia during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down in Thalmann.

The most significant damage was reported near the Stanley Maxwell and Rehoboth Rd intersection.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms formed in the afternoon due to a sea breeze and a mid-level disturbance that moved in from the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McIntosh Emergency Manager reported trees and power lines down along Highway 17, near the Libery County border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms formed in the afternoon due to a sea breeze and a mid-level disturbance that moved in from the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Georgia Department of Natural Resources reported numerous trees down across the south end of Sapelo Island, including a 100 year old 60 ft oak tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms formed in the afternoon due to a sea breeze and a mid-level disturbance that moved in from the west.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Partial canopy collapse at the Circle K gas station on Highway 21.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low crossed the area with 500 mb temps in the -14/-15 deg C range. This instability coupled with daytime heating and passing upper level short wave trough energy spawned a few strong to severe storms over the area during the late evening in advance of an approaching cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down over Mark Mobly Road. The time of damage was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was lifting over north central Florida. Strong upper level winds created enhanced divergence over the area and combined with large lift in the hail growth zone, early morning supercells produced large hail over SE Ga. As the day progressed, upper level forcing converged with the warm front over NE FL to produce a long-lived supercell storm that track along and just south of the Interstate 10 corridor from late morning through the mid afternoon hours. This cell produced widespread wind damage and large hail throughout its track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported in Willacoochee.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was lifting over north central Florida. Strong upper level winds created enhanced divergence over the area and combined with large lift in the hail growth zone, early morning supercells produced large hail over SE Ga. As the day progressed, upper level forcing converged with the warm front over NE FL to produce a long-lived supercell storm that track along and just south of the Interstate 10 corridor from late morning through the mid afternoon hours. This cell produced widespread wind damage and large hail throughout its track.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size was reported SSE over Douglas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold front that was across the mid-Mississippi Valley on the 13th had moved into Georgia early on the 14th, continuing to shift south through the day.  A very warm and unstable tropical airmass was in place ahead of the front, and several showers and thunderstorms developed.  A couple of thunderstorms produced intense rainfall and flash flooding in two location of central Georgia.  The second highest daily rainfall on record of 5.73 inches was recorded at the Columbus Metropolitan Airport in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County Police reported several roads under water around 2 miles southwest of Dublin. A drainage branch was overflowing a portion of Southern Pines Road near U.S. Highway 441. Also several cars were reported partially submerged by the local media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Southeast remained in an active northwest flow pattern on the 1st, with an upper level disturbance moving through the area.  A weak surface trough remained in place across the state as well.  Showers and thunderstorms developed early in the morning as a result of the disturbance and tropical moisture already in place over the state.  A combination of back-building and training storms over the same areas caused intense rainfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches in less than 8 hours. This caused significant flash flooding in Pickens and Gilmer counties of north Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pickens County Fire Department evacuated a man from a manufactured home off Wigington Lane north of Jasper due to a creek beginning to flood on the property. The flood waters damaged a car in front of the house and entered the home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Southeast remained in an active northwest flow pattern on the 7th, with yet another upper level disturbance moving through the area.  Similar to what happened on August 1st, showers and thunderstorms developed early in the morning on the 7th as a result of the disturbance, with anywhere from 5 to 10 inches of rain leading to significant flash flooding across portions of north Georgia, in some of the same areas that were affected on the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An off duty NWS employee reported the Sullivan Branch was out of its banks and caused minor flooding on the Summergrove golf course.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak surface ridge remained in place across north Georgia, with a stalled surface boundary draped across central Georgia.  A very warm and moist airmass remained in place across the area.  Another front pushed across the Ohio Valley and into the Appalachians during the day on the 23rd.  Widespread showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours, with several reports of damaging winds and isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Department of Transportation reported several trees down along Highway 11 south of Macon.  Trees were blocking lanes near Avondale Mill Road.

Thunderstorms with heavy rain in northwest and northeast Georgia produced isolated flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Southeast remained in an active northwest flow pattern on the 1st, with an upper level disturbance moving through the area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height of a USGS stream gage on the Cartecay River near East Ellijay reached flood stage of 7 feet. The river crest at 10.2 feet causing significant flooding. The Gilmer County emergency manager indicated around 7 roads were either flooded or damaged in the East Ellijay area by high water from the Cartecay River. Around 25 structures were flooded with some evacuations on White Water Lane, Bernhardt Road, River Lane and Riverside Drive. The East Ellijay Police Chief was injured by electric shock during an evacuation on Riverside Drive. A power line fell on the patrol car as it was passing through the flooded road. He was hospitalized and released a day or two later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak warm front was stalled across central Georgia early on the 18th, and lifted north during the day as a wedge of cool high pressure remained in place across northeast Georgia.  An area of low pressure along the Louisiana coast also drifted northeast during the day.  All these factors combined to produce several showers and thunderstorms across all but northeast Georgia, with isolated damaging wind reports.  Isolated flash flooding occurred in south Toombs County due to intense rainfall. Because of the low-level shear associated with the approaching surface low from Louisiana, one thunderstorm was briefly able to spin up a weak tornado in Heard County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported several trees down along Linton Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of passing upper level energy over an unstable airmass and low level convergence form the sea breezes and outflow boundaries resulted in severe storms across the forecast area. A surface front continued to meander over SE Ga/NE Fl.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down in Douglas.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team determined that an EF0 tornado touched down just inside the Georgia-Alabama state line in Heard County.  A mobile home sustained roof damage and its porch was destroyed as the tornado tracked northeastward, paralleling Upper Caldwell Road as it strengthened to an EF1.  The most significant damage was as the tornado crossed Upper Caldwell Road, snapping dozens of trees.  The tornado left a path 0.4 miles in length, weakening back to an EF0 with tree limbs down before it lifted.  |[08/18/13: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF1, Heard, 2013:013].

Similar to what happened on August 1st, showers and thunderstorms developed early in the morning on the 7th as a result of the disturbance, with anywhere from 5 to 10 inches of rain leading to significant flash flooding across portions of north Georgia, in some of the same areas that were affected on the 1st.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height at a USGS stream gage on the Talking Rock Creek near Hinton reached flood stage of 10 feet. The creek crest at 11.8 feet causing minor flooding of creek access roads and woodlands.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak frontal boundary remained stalled over central Georgia on the 21st.  A warm and moist airmass remained in place, and once again several showers and thunderstorms developed.  A couple of these produced isolated flash flooding in north Georgia, and one briefly became severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The DeKalb County emergency manager reported the South Fork Peachtree Creek came out of its banks and caused minor flooding near Orion Drive and Lawrenceville Highway. The same creek also flooded portions of Medlock Park and athletic fields with up to 3 feet of water. Three homes on Scott Circle near the creek had flood waters enter their crawl spaces. A portion of Snapfinger Creek came out of its banks and flooded a home on Rowland Road with around one inch of water.  The stage height at a USGS stream gage on Stone Mountain Creek at Silver Hill Road reached flood stage of 9 feet. The creek crest at 9.1 feet. Minor flooding occurred in the woodlands and residential yards along the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low was in place across the Great Lakes, allowing continued intermittent upper level disturbances to rotate around and affect the Southeast.  A complicated surface pattern in place with multiple boundaries left over from previous days' convection as well as a weak nearly stationary front, combined with ample moisture and high levels of instability provided plenty of focus for thunderstorm development.  Numerous storms did indeed develop, and many of these became severe with mainly damaging winds reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave moved from the Ohio Valley into the Mid Atlantic States during the day.  A surface cold front pushed into north Georgia ahead of the shortwave during the morning, progressing into central Georgia during the afternoon hours.  Temperatures climbed into the 90s across most of the area with strong instability in place as well as deep layer shear, supportive of severe thunderstorm development.  Numerous severe thunderstorms did indeed develop with mainly downed trees and some large hail, but two small tornadoes also touched down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Union County 911 Center reported that numerous trees were down countywide, with many taking down power lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team determined that the tornado that began in Cherokee County and passed through Cobb County lifted in Fulton County.  It moved over the Chattahoochee River near Morgan Falls, retaining its EF-1 strength as it snapped and uprooted dozens of trees along the riverbank.  It damaged roofs at the Laurel at Overlook Park Apartments.  Netting polls at the driving range at the Steel Canyon Golf Club were damaged, with the netting ripped off or tangled.  The tornado continued southeast, snapping or uprooting trees until it finally lifted just short of the DeKalb County line, where it snapped and uprooted a few trees along Twin Branch Road.|[06/13/13: Tornado #2, County #3-3, EF1, Cherokee-Cobb-Fulton, 2013:012].

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that touched down just inside the Cherokee County line moved into Cobb County and strengthened to an EF-1.  The tornado tracked southeast through Cobb County with the most significant damage occurring near Wendwood Drive Northeast and near Bishop Lake where dozens of trees were snapped and uprooted, many causing damage to homes.  The tornado continued to track southeast, and crossed the Chattahoochee River at the Cobb-Fulton County line near the Morgan Falls Dam.|[06/13/13: Tornado #2, County #2-3, EF1, Cherokee-Cobb-Fulton, 2013:012].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initially, multi-cell thunderstorms developed inland within an unstable environment featuring MLCAPES between 2500-3500 j/kg and a well defined shortwave trough just north/northwest of the forecast area. These thunderstorms shifted south/southeast toward the coast, prompting warnings for inland counties, while other thunderstorms developed near the seabreeze pinned at the coast. Eventually, complex mesoscale boundaries developed and dominated convective initiation as well as the growth and propagation of thunderstorms, supporting occasional episodes of severe weather especially within coastal counties from Charleston to McIntosh. The most intense thunderstorm produced golf ball size hail fell in Meridian in McIntosh County. We also received other reports of quarter size hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported one tree uprooted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of moist southwesterly flow ahead of a deep trough aloft and a tropical low in the Gulf of Mexico brought copious amounts of moisture to the state on the 5th and 6th.  On the 6th, the upper trough continued to dig, allowing a cold front to approach, which served to focus convection.  Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed through the period, with a few of these producing enough rain for flash flooding.  One thunderstorm was briefly able to reach severe limits in Atlanta, with several reports of trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense heavy rainfall of 3.23 inches in 100 minutes at the Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport caused significant flooding on portions of Interstate 285 at the Camp Creek Parkway intersection. Several cars stalled out in the flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of moist southwesterly flow ahead of a deep trough aloft and a tropical low in the Gulf of Mexico brought copious amounts of moisture to the state on the 5th and 6th.  On the 6th, the upper trough continued to dig, allowing a cold front to approach, which served to focus convection.  Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed through the period, with a few of these producing enough rain for flash flooding.  One thunderstorm was briefly able to reach severe limits in Atlanta, with several reports of trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around 3.5 inches of rain in less than 3 hours caused a pond to overflow Sunset Blvd. Rapidly flowing water caused severe erosion and dug a six foot deep hole in front of a culvert pipe on Grandchester Way.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea off the South Carolina Coast along with the remains of a weak frontal boundary combined to produce a new round of showers and thunderstorms across the area.  Several thunderstorms reached severe levels with downed trees reported across north and central Georgia.  The heaviest rain was confined to mainly east central Georgia, in closer proximity to Andrea.  A couple of these storms produced enough rainfall to result in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dodge County Skywarn Coordinator relayed spotter reports of several trees down just northeast of Eastman-Dodge County Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Initially, multi-cell thunderstorms developed inland within an unstable environment featuring MLCAPES between 2500-3500 j/kg and a well defined shortwave trough just north/northwest of the forecast area. These thunderstorms shifted south/southeast toward the coast, prompting warnings for inland counties, while other thunderstorms developed near the seabreeze pinned at the coast. Eventually, complex mesoscale boundaries developed and dominated convective initiation as well as the growth and propagation of thunderstorms, supporting occasional episodes of severe weather especially within coastal counties from Charleston to McIntosh. The most intense thunderstorm produced golf ball size hail fell in Meridian in McIntosh County. We also received other reports of quarter size hail and wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter size hail near Meridian.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low was in place across the Great Lakes, allowing continued intermittent upper level disturbances to rotate around and affect the Southeast.  A complicated surface pattern in place with multiple boundaries left over from previous days' convection as well as a weak nearly stationary front, combined with ample moisture and high levels of instability provided plenty of focus for thunderstorm development.  Numerous storms did indeed develop, and many of these became severe with mainly damaging winds reported.

A strong closed surface low moved through the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys on the 5th.  This pushed a cold front across Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours.  A squall line ahead of the front state, with damage occurring in northwest Georgia in the early afternoon and continuing into central Georgia through the mid-evening hours.  Widespread wind damage, mainly in the form of fallen trees, occurred, but one tornado was observed as well in Glascock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Muscogee County 911 Center reported that numerous trees were blown down or snapped in the northern part of the county, including in north Columbus.  Numerous power outages were reported as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large storm system approached the state on the 30th.  A highly amplified upper trough over the plains pushed east during the day, dragging a strong cold front through the Southeast.  A squall line associated with the cold front pushed into northwest and west central Georgia during the mid-morning hours, and pushed through the rest of the state through the afternoon and evening.  Widespread wind damage occurred with this line as it moved through.  Additionally, a supercell thunderstorm ahead of the line spawned two tornadoes; a rare January EF-3 across Bartow and Gordon Counties and an EF-1 across Gilmer and Fannin Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado that first touched down in Bartow County continued into Gordon County.  Numerous trees and power lines were downed from Redbud to Highway 53 and seven poultry homes were destroyed.  In all, 268 homes were impacted: 30 were completely destroyed, 110 had major damage, and another 70 had minor damage.  Of these, 202 were single family homes and 66 were mobile homes.  Eight injuries were also reported which all occurred in one home.  Some of the EF-3 damage in Gordon County occurred literally only blocks away from EF-3 damage that occurred on December 22, 2011.|[01/30/13: Tornado #1, County #2-2, EF3, Bartow-Gordon, 2013:001].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A dry cold front pushed through the area on the 1st, bringing a cold airmass to north and central Georgia.  High pressure built in behind the front, and pushed offshore on Saturday the 2nd.  An upper level clipper disturbance pushed through the Ohio Valley on the 2nd as well.  Meanwhile, two weak surface boundaries moved through the area, one on Saturday afternoon and the other Saturday night, both of which brought shallow moisture to the area.  With northwest winds, uplift as a result of topography enhanced the clipper system, and the resulting precipitation changed over to snow across portions of north Georgia.  In the end, snowfall accumulation totaled generally betwee 3 to 5 inches, mainly across the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White County Emergency Manager and 911 Call Center reported that the snow began falling in the late morning and had accumulated to around 3 to 4 inches across the northern part of the county by about 700 PM.  The higher elevations had up to 5 inches of snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down off of Chapman Drive and snapped or uprooted hundreds of softwood and hardwood trees as it moved northeast.  Eight power poles were snapped in the Bushyhead Road area.  Up to 12,000 people were without power.  Maximum wind speeds in Gilmer County were up to 90 MPH.  The tornado then moved into Fannin County.|[01/30/13: Tornado #2, County #1-2, EF1, Gilmer-Fannin, 2013:002].

Thirty structures were damaged mainly due to falling trees.  While the maximum path width was about a half mile wide, the majority of the path was 300-400 yards wide.|[01/30/13: Tornado #2, County #2-2, EF1, Gilmer-Fannin, 2013:002].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system combined with a moist south to southeast flow, moderate instability, a cold pool aloft, and enhanced surface convergence along and ahead of an approaching cold front moving through the Deep South.  This combination of parameters resulted in widespread severe weather across northwest, west central, and central Georgia including damaging winds, large hail, and a tornado.  A wedge of cold air was in place across northeast Georgia, which acted to stabilize this area with only isolated reports of severe weather.  The bulk of the severe weather was concentrated along a zone just to the south and west of the wedge front, where instability and low-level shear were maximized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS station at the Macon airport measured a 52 kt gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms ahead of an approaching cold front started developing and intensifying in the mid to late afternoon hours across northern Georgia and Alabama. There were also discrete supercells developing ahead of the main line during peak heating. The line approached the western part of the forecast area around 9 pm as the line was beginning to interact and consolidate with the discrete cells out ahead of it. The line swept across the entire forecast area with numerous reports of wind damage and a smaller collection of large hail. The near storm environment was marked by impressive mid levels with 500-700 mb lapse rates less than -7 C/km and LI's as low as -3 to -4 C. The presence of an elevated mixed layer resulted in DCAPE values in the 800-1000 J/kg range. The upstream convective line was forced by strong ambient shear (on the order of 50-60 kts in the 0-6 km layer) with adequate surface based instability (CAPE in the 500-750 J/kg range). The supercell elements upstream yielded a tornado threat which subsequently diminished into the forecast area with less instability present and a convective mode favoring linear segments.  The Storm Prediction Center hoisted a Severe Thunderstorm Watch around 7:30 pm for all of southeast Georgia and the portion of South Carolina that bordered the Savannah river. The line not only maintained its strength but intensified with base velocities upwards of 70 knots at times as it entered the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public estimated a 70 mph wind gust.

This combination of parameters resulted in widespread severe weather across northwest, west central, and central Georgia including damaging winds, large hail, and a tornado.  A wedge of cold air was in place across northeast Georgia, which acted to stabilize this area with only isolated reports of severe weather.  The bulk of the severe weather was concentrated along a zone just to the south and west of the wedge front, where instability and low-level shear were maximized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County 911 Center reported that numerous trees were blown down or snapped, causing several power outages in Summerville.  Some of the trees fell on homes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly shifted south over portions of southeast Georgia during overnight hours.  A few thunderstorms developed along the front as it lifted back northward as a warm front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported dime to nickel size hail on Chatham Parkway south of Interstate 16.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms ahead of an approaching cold front started developing and intensifying in the mid to late afternoon hours across northern Georgia and Alabama. There were also discrete supercells developing ahead of the main line during peak heating. The line approached the western part of the forecast area around 9 pm as the line was beginning to interact and consolidate with the discrete cells out ahead of it. The line swept across the entire forecast area with numerous reports of wind damage and a smaller collection of large hail. The near storm environment was marked by impressive mid levels with 500-700 mb lapse rates less than -7 C/km and LI's as low as -3 to -4 C. The presence of an elevated mixed layer resulted in DCAPE values in the 800-1000 J/kg range. The upstream convective line was forced by strong ambient shear (on the order of 50-60 kts in the 0-6 km layer) with adequate surface based instability (CAPE in the 500-750 J/kg range). The supercell elements upstream yielded a tornado threat which subsequently diminished into the forecast area with less instability present and a convective mode favoring linear segments.  The Storm Prediction Center hoisted a Severe Thunderstorm Watch around 7:30 pm for all of southeast Georgia and the portion of South Carolina that bordered the Savannah river. The line not only maintained its strength but intensified with base velocities upwards of 70 knots at times as it entered the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Effingham County 911 Call Center reported half dollar size hail in Clyo, Georgia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a cold front moved south into the region and stalled, a series of upper level disturbances moved through the area with broad southwest flow and moist conditions.  As the boundary became oriented parallel to the upper level winds, training bands of convection set up, particularly across southwest Georgia where flash flooding and areal flooding occurred.  Radar estimated that rainfall amounts of 8-10 inches occurred in a swath from Early county northeastward to Ben Hill county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rain over a greater than 6 hour period led to flooding across portions of Irwin County.  Around a half dozen dirt roads were closed around the county as well as portions of Highway 107.  Irwin county schools were closed for the day as a result of the flooding.  No structures were impacted.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported golfball sized  hail in Eatonton.  The hail was so deep it covered the ground completely.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that an EF2 tornado with wind speeds up to 120 MPH touched down near Rocky Mount Road and Caldwell Road in Meriwether County.  IT traveled east to southeast before lifting one mile west of Concord in Pike County.  Total path length was 13.35 miles and up to 300 yards wide.  In Meriwether County, thousands of trees were either blown over or snapped, with some falling on homes.|[03/18/13: Tornado #1, County #1-2, EF2, Meriwether-Pike, 2013:004].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of widespread light to moderate rain moved across much of southeast South Carolina and southeast Georgia during the overnight and early morning hours. Increased rainfall rates within embedded thunderstorms caused excessive amounts and eventually flash flooding around the Savannah metro area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chatham County Emergency Manager reported Grange Road closed between Highway 21 and Highway 25 due to flooding.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County Emergency Manager reported that widespread trees were down across the county.  Nearly all unincorporated areas were without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A back door dry cold front moved through the state early on the 8th.  An upper ridge was slowly building across the area as well, leading to dry conditions, although relative humidity values remained above critical levels for dangerous fire weather conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Forest Service reported that a wildfire of unknown cause labeled May Mtn. burned 3 acres and resulted in $250 in damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of upper level disturbance rotating around a mean longwave trough west of the area brought copious amounts of moisture into the area.  The KTAE rawinsonde sounding on the evening of February 22nd measured a PWAT value of 1.77 inches, which was the 2nd highest PWAT observed during the month of February at that location.  Rainfall amounts from near 5 inches to excess of 13 inches were observed during the 5 day period between Feb 22-26.  This led to areas of flooding.  A couple of severe storms also occurred during the early morning hours of Feb 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Persistent moderate to heavy rainfall over a long duration led to significant flooding across the county.  Several dozen roads were closed across the county, and water affected several homes on Willow Road in the western part of the county near Ponds.  The water was about 1.5 feet up the skirting of the house porches.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer shear, steep mid-level lapse rates, and sufficient instability came together to produce several severe thunderstorms across the area.  Large hail was the primary threat, although some isolated occurrences of wind damage also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar size hail was reported about 1 mile south of Coolidge.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail covered the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported via WALB's facebook page.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was reported via WALB's facebook page on Highway 76 about 3 miles west of Nashville.

Deep layer shear, steep mid-level lapse rates, and sufficient instability came together to produce several severe thunderstorms across the area.  Large hail was the primary threat, although some isolated occurrences of wind damage also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were blown down within the Nashville city limits.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms ahead of an approaching cold front started developing and intensifying in the mid to late afternoon hours across northern Georgia and Alabama. There were also discrete supercells developing ahead of the main line during peak heating. The line approached the western part of the forecast area around 9 pm as the line was beginning to interact and consolidate with the discrete cells out ahead of it. The line swept across the entire forecast area with numerous reports of wind damage and a smaller collection of large hail. The near storm environment was marked by impressive mid levels with 500-700 mb lapse rates less than -7 C/km and LI's as low as -3 to -4 C. The presence of an elevated mixed layer resulted in DCAPE values in the 800-1000 J/kg range. The upstream convective line was forced by strong ambient shear (on the order of 50-60 kts in the 0-6 km layer) with adequate surface based instability (CAPE in the 500-750 J/kg range). The supercell elements upstream yielded a tornado threat which subsequently diminished into the forecast area with less instability present and a convective mode favoring linear segments.  The Storm Prediction Center hoisted a Severe Thunderstorm Watch around 7:30 pm for all of southeast Georgia and the portion of South Carolina that bordered the Savannah river. The line not only maintained its strength but intensified with base velocities upwards of 70 knots at times as it entered the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bulloch County 911 Call Center reported a tree down near the intersection of Rocky Ford Road and Highway 25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A well-defined upper shortwave pushed through the Deep South in late on the 11th.  A strong surface low over the Central Plains early in the day began lifting to the northeast toward the Great Lakes, dragging the associated cold front moved into northwest Georgia late in the day.  Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of the front, with several reports of large hail, including a couple of reports of up to baseball sized hail and several reports of golfball sized hail.  Three weak tornadoes occurred as well, two EF-1s and an EF-0.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that an EF-1 tornado touched down in Lumpkin County along Gold Ridge Road where numerous trees were snapped or uprooted.  One small outbuilding had its roof blown off and two homes sustained minor roof damage.  The tornado tracked northeast crossing Evergreen Court where trees were snapped and uprooted.  The tornado reached maximum intensity, around 105 MPH, shortly after and along Bridgestone Way where it paralleled the road.  Numerous hardwood and softwood trees were snapped and uprooted.  Some of the trees fell onto homes.  Twelve homes were damaged on Bridgestone Way, with two of them destroyed by trees.  The tornado then continued into Hall County.|[04/11/13: Tornado #3, County #1-3, EF1, Lumpkin-Hall-Lumpkin, 2013:007].

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team determined that the tornado that touched down in Lumpkin County crossed over into Hall County briefly.  It crossed Old Whelchel Road, snapping a few trees and downing powerlines.  Two homes sustained very minor roof damage.|[04/11/13: Tornado #3, County #2-3, EF1, Lumpkin-Hall-Lumpkin, 2013:007].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening of the 14th, a strong shortwave moved across the lower Mississippi Valley, bringing widespread rain to portions of north and central Georgia.  Behind this rain, a wake low developed and moved across central Georgia, bringing wind gusts between 35 and 45 MPH.  Because of the saturated soils, a few trees came down with these strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Amateur radio operators reported that a tree fell on a house in Eatonton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public and broadcast media reported large hail 1 to 2 inches in diameter across the Columbus area.  The hail fell for 10 minutes and covered the ground.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service survey team determined that an EF-2 tornado that touched down in Meriwether County continued into Pike County.  A home sustained minor roof damage, had both doors blown out, and four front windows were damaged.  One barn was completely destroyed, and another shifted several feet off its foundation.  A pontoon boat was thrown into a third barn.  Two neighboring houses sustained minor damage, one to the roof and another to the garage.  Thousands of trees were snapped or uprooted, some falling on homes.|[03/18/13: Tornado #1, County #2-2, EF2, Meriwether-Pike, 2013:004].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front lifted north over the region as a surface low tracked from the northern Gulf of Mexico to the southeast coast.  A shortwave accompanied the surface low and helped produced a few thunderstorms over southeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media reported penny size hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Glascock County Emergency Manager reported that numerous trees and power lines were blown down from the Mitchell area to the Edgehill area.  Several large pines and at least one power pole were snapped.  Two homes were damaged by falling trees and several roads were blocked.  A tin roof was ripped off of a mobile home, and the south end of the county was without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 26th, an area of low pressure was located in the MidSouth, with a cold front stretching south across eastern Mississippi and a warm front stretching southeast toward the Georgia/Florida border.  The warm front lifted north through the day as the cold front pushed east.  Another round of significant heavy rainfall occurred during the morning hours, and the rain-cooled air behind the rainfall led to the development of a wake low across eastern Alabama.  As this low moved across Georgia, winds increased enough to bring down isolated trees, especially since the soils were saturated as a result of the recent rains.  One tree fell on a car, killing the occupant.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Rockdale County Sheriff's Office reported that a large pine tree fell on a pickup truck traveling on Interstate 20 between Sigman Road and the DeKalb County line, killing the occupant.  This accident resulted in a multiple-car pileup, as at least six other cars were involved in crashes as they tried to avoid the truck and the tree. The winds at the time were east at 15 to 20 MPH with gusts to 30 MPH and were associated with a wake low pressure gradient.  Saturated soils due to recent heavy rainfall contributed to the tree falling.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upson County 911 Center reported that trees and power lines were blown down county-wide, especially in the Yatesville area.  A mobile home was destroyed by a falling tree on Manley Road.  A vacant residence on Johnston Drive was damaged by a falling tree, and another home on Tom McKinley Road sustained roof and structural damage from a falling tree.  Numerous homes had shingle damage.  Farm equipment was blown over at The Rock Ranch.  Two to three chicken houses were blown apart.  A hangar door was blown off its tracks at the Thomaston-Upson airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 6th, surface high pressure was beginning to build into the area, with plentiful low-level moisture in place for the formation of patchy areas of very dense fog.  Visibilities across central Georgia were reduced to a quarter mile or less in several areas.  Lingering smoke combined with an area of dense fog to reduce visibility to less than 100 feet on Interstate 16 in far western Laurens county. These extremely poor visibilities led to a multiple-vehicle pileup at this location, with 27 vehicles involved, seven of them commercial trucks.  One of the trucks was an empty fuel tanker; fumes inside the tanker exploded and caught fire.  The accident resulted in four fatalities and at least nine injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Georgia State Patrol and several media outlets reported a multiple-vehicle accident along Interstate 16 at mile marker 38 near the Laurens/Twiggs county line.  Twenty-seven vehicles, seven of them commercial trucks, were involved in the accident, which resulted in four fatalities and at least nine injuries.
