EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down on Highway 309 near Baxley Road.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey team determined that an EF-0 tornado touched down just south of Interstate 20 near Chandler Road.  The tornado continued northeast, crossing Kings Gin Road and along Thomas Road, snapping and uprooting several dozen trees.  Three homes on Kings Gin Road sustained minor roof damage where shingles or a portion of the roof was peeled back.  The tornado crossed Interstate 20, knocking over several trees on both east and westbound sides, just east of where Old Georgia 100 crosses the interstate.  The tornado continued across Dr. King Road and Petty Road, snapping and uprooting trees and just missing a large manufacturing center.  The tornado continued northeast and began to weaken as it headed across State Highway 100, Daniel Road, and U.S. Highway 78.  Several trees were uprooted and snapped along that path and a flower shop sustained minor roof damage at the intersection of U.S. Highway 78 and Old Ridgeway Road.  The tornado lifted shortly after crossing Highway 78.  |[04/11/13: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF0, Haralson, 2013:005].

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service storm survey determined that a second brief tornado touched down associated with the same supercell thunderstorm as the Haralson County tornado.  The tornado touched down near railroad tracks just south of Coots Lake Road where some trees were uprooted.  From this point the tornado traveled to the north-northeast.  The most damage occurred in a wooded area 0.3 miles north of where the tornado touched down.  At this point approximately 50 trees were snapped and about 25 trees were uprooted.  The tornado dissipated in an open area just short of a residence.  Damage was confined to trees.|[04/11/13: Tornado #2, County #1-1, EF1, Polk, 2013:006].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 24th, a warm front was situated along the Georgia/Florida border, with an area of surface low pressure along the Florida Panhandle.  The surface low worked its way slowly east and northeast along the warm front as it lifted north into central Georgia during the morning hours.  A shortwave aloft pushed through the Southeast, helping provide lift for the convection that developed.  Several thunderstorms developed during the morning hours; some reached severe levels with mainly hail and some tree damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County Emergency Manager reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave moved across the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys on the 31st, and with weak to moderate instability in place across central Georgia, several thunderstorms developed.  A couple of these thunderstorms reached severe levels with large hail reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Weak upper level ridging persisted across the area on the 17th.  A shortwave had moved through the area the previous day, and the remnants of a mesoscale convective system (MCS) that moved through West Virginia as a result had worked its way south-southwest into eastern Tennessee and western North Carolina during the day on the 17th.  With surface flow to the southwest and flow aloft to the northeast, the storms that developed along the outflow from the remnant MCS moved very little.  Thunderstorms that developed in the north Georgia mountains during the late afternoon and evening remained nearly stationary, resulting in flash flooding in Fannin and Union Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: County officials reported that Anderson and Coosa Creeks flooded the Mountain Oak Cabins and Campground area around 3.5 miles southwest of Blairsville. The RV park was flooded in low lying areas with a few vacation homes inundated with water. A few culverts were washed out. The Youngcane and Jones Creeks flooded portions of Byers Road around 7 miles west of Blairsville. The flood waters topped the bridge and caused erosion damage to it.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level system combined with a moist south to southeast flow, moderate instability, a cold pool aloft, and enhanced surface convergence along and ahead of an approaching cold front moving through the Deep South.  This combination of parameters resulted in widespread severe weather across northwest, west central, and central Georgia including damaging winds, large hail, and a tornado.  A wedge of cold air was in place across northeast Georgia, which acted to stabilize this area with only isolated reports of severe weather.  The bulk of the severe weather was concentrated along a zone just to the south and west of the wedge front, where instability and low-level shear were maximized.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Henry County Emergency Manager, current and former National Weather Service Employees, and the public reported quarter to baseball sized hail in the second of two significant hailstorms across Henry County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed surface low moved through the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys on the 5th.  This pushed a cold front across Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours.  A squall line ahead of the front state, with damage occurring in northwest Georgia in the early afternoon and continuing into central Georgia through the mid-evening hours.  Widespread wind damage, mainly in the form of fallen trees, occurred, but one tornado was observed as well in Glascock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A National Weather Service Storm Survey determined that an EF-1 tornado touched down  northwest of Gibson.   Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted along the 1.5 mile path.  As the tornado crossed Georgia State Highway 171, it ripped off 20 percent of the metal roof from a home residence, tossed a camper trailer 50 feet, and knocked two mobile homes off their foundations.  The occupant of one home had to be rescued.  The worst damage occurred near the intersection of Georgia Highway 171 and Magnolia Church Road where 60 percent of the Magnolia Baptist Church roof was ripped off, and the brick-walled fellowship hall on the west end of the church was completely destroyed.  Numerous tombstones and other structures at the adjoining cemetery near the church were also damaged.|[03/05/13: Tornado #1, County #1-1, EF1, Glascock, 2013:003].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper level troughing and a weak surface front affected the region for a couple of days at the end of the month.  Ample instability contributed to the development of a few severe storms with damaging winds the main threat.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There was significant roof damage to a home with windows blown out.  Several pecan trees and two large oaks trees were also blown down.  Monetary damage was estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered severe storms developed over NE Florida during the late afternoon over an unstable and moist airmass where the sea breezes and outflows converged. During the evening, and upper level disturbance over SE Georgia fueled severe storms where outflows converged.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buildings were damaged by thunderstorm winds. One mobile home was damaged due to the roof being blown off at 1394 Hortense Road. Two barns were destroyed by thunderstorm winds. One barn was located at 235 Horsestomp Road. The other barn was located at 2078 Old Highway 32. At 11766 Raybon Road West a tree was blown over and landed on a carport with the car still underneath. At 1597 Highway 32 West, trees uprooted and caused damage to pool concrete. A wooden fence, wire fence, and gate were also damaged. The time of wind damage was estimated and based on radar imagery. The cost of damage was estimated...and is likely an underestimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large storm system approached the state on the 30th.  A highly amplified upper trough over the plains pushed east during the day, dragging a strong cold front through the Southeast.  A squall line associated with the cold front pushed into northwest and west central Georgia during the mid-morning hours, and pushed through the rest of the state through the afternoon and evening.  Widespread wind damage occurred with this line as it moved through.  Additionally, a supercell thunderstorm ahead of the line spawned two tornadoes; a rare January EF-3 across Bartow and Gordon Counties and an EF-1 across Gilmer and Fannin Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just southwest of Adairsville at approximately 1112 AM EST.  The tornado strengthened to high-end EF-3 in Adairsville where dozens of homes were damaged or destroyed.  At least 95 structures were damaged, with 31 destroyed, 17 sustaining major damage, and 47 with minor damage.  Most of the damage to homes was a result of trees falling on homes in downtown Adairsville.  The worst damage was along Highway 140 where a large portion of a manufacturing plant was destroyed.  One fatality occurred when a tree fell on a mobile home.  Over 100 cars were overturned near exit 306 on Interstate 75.|[01/30/13: Tornado #1, County #1-2, EF3, Bartow-Gordon, 2013:001].

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of January 24, a strong area of high pressure set up over the eastern Carolinas, ridging into north and central Georgia.  Accompanying this high pressure were cold temperatures, falling into the 30s and then to the mid and upper 20s through early Friday morning, January 25.  A weak cold front and associated precipitation pushed across the Southeast on the 25th.  With the cold air in place at the surface and precipitation moving across the area, freezing rain fell across portions of north Georgia, with the highest accumulations experienced across northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began just after sunrise on the morning of the 25th, accumulating on elevated surfaces, bridges, and overpasses through the morning and afternoon hours.  Widespread amounts of 0.10 to 0.25 inches of freezing rain were reported.  The slick roads resulted in numerous automobile accidents and power outages across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the conclusion of the heavy rainfall event on the 16th, a strong upper level low moved across the Southeast, basically across the Interstate 20 corridor.  Several light bands of snow fell particularly north and west of the track of the upper low on the 17th.  While snow was expected across a larger portion of north Georgia, the upper level low began to rapidly weaken and thus the event was mainly rain.  However, some light snow did fall west of the Atlanta area, and black ice was reported on the morning of the 18th.  As the upper level low tracked across, winds increased across the area.  With the saturated soils in place from the week of heavy rain, with several trees blew down across Barrow and Banks Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Around an inch of snow fell across the county during the evening hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong system affected the area at the end of January with unusually strong wind fields.  The 500 mb winds were as high as 110 knots with the upper level trough, and 850 mb winds reached around 70 knots in the local area. These very strong low level winds combined with SBCAPE values of 300-500 j/kg produced widespread straight line wind damage across the area with numerous trees and power lines blown down as a squall line moved through the area.  Strong non-convective related winds also occurred ahead of the main line of convection and resulted in a few trees being blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A deputy reported nickle size hail in Warwick.

With the cold air in place at the surface and precipitation moving across the area, freezing rain fell across portions of north Georgia, with the highest accumulations experienced across northeast Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Freezing rain began just after sunrise on the morning of the 25th, accumulating on elevated surfaces, bridges, and overpasses through the morning and afternoon hours.  Widespread amounts of 0.10 to 0.25 inches of freezing rain were reported.  The slick roads resulted in numerous automobile accidents and power outages across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: At the conclusion of the heavy rainfall event on the 16th, a strong upper level low moved across the Southeast, basically across the Interstate 20 corridor.  Several light bands of snow fell particularly north and west of the track of the upper low on the 17th.  While snow was expected across a larger portion of north Georgia, the upper level low began to rapidly weaken and thus the event was mainly rain.  However, some light snow did fall west of the Atlanta area, and black ice was reported on the morning of the 18th.  As the upper level low tracked across, winds increased across the area.  With the saturated soils in place from the week of heavy rain, with several trees blew down across Barrow and Banks Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds brought down about half a dozen trees across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The cold front that was across the mid-Mississippi Valley on the 13th had moved into Georgia early on the 14th, continuing to shift south through the day.  A very warm and unstable tropical airmass was in place ahead of the front, and several showers and thunderstorms developed.  A couple of thunderstorms produced intense rainfall and flash flooding in two location of central Georgia.  The second highest daily rainfall on record of 5.73 inches was recorded at the Columbus Metropolitan Airport in the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Muscogee County officials reported the Cooper Creek Reservoir around 5 miles northeast of Columbus was overflowing and flooding Cooper Creek downstream from the dam. At least four roads were affected by flood waters and closed such as Fairview Drive, Roland Avenue, Northgate Drive and Reese Road. Flood waters entered a home in the Northgate subdivision and heavily damaged culvert pipes under residential driveways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms developed across Southeast Georgia during afternoon heating. These thunderstorms were nearly stationary and produced locally excessive rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reported flooding at the Georgia Villas near Georgia Southern University, relayed by broadcast media. There is an estimated two to three feet of standing water and partially submerged vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated severe storm blew down a couple of trees near Bainbridge.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet pattern developed across the area during the middle of August with persistent rounds of showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe.  The heavy rainfall saturated grounds and led to instances of flooding and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down, including one over power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across Southeast Georgia starting in the evening and overnight hours. Strong winds developed in associated with the strong pressure gradient and the presence of a strong low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree reported down across Wilkinson Road at Rye Patch Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved across Southeast Georgia starting in the evening and overnight hours. Strong winds developed in associated with the strong pressure gradient and the presence of a strong low level jet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One tree down on Mill Pond Road off Highway 84.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated strong to severe thunderstorms developed in the late afternoon hours in association with a nearby complex low pressure system and attending cold front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds associated with a thunderstorm shifted a double-wide mobile home a few feet off its foundation. Also, the winds tore the siding off an outbuilding and snapped off, uprooted, or broke off large branches from approximately two dozen trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was located over southern Kentucky on the morning of the 19th, with a shortwave trough extending into northern Georgia. Tropical moisture prevailed over Georgia as well.  Widespread showers with embedded thunderstorms developed across a portion of north Georgia early in the morning and moved slowly south. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches occurred in less than 6 hours prior to 700 AM EST in an area from Dawsonville to Gainesville to Lawrenceville to Roswell. Significant flash flooding occurred with major damage to roads and bridges near Flowery Branch. Another 3 to 7 inches of rain occurred in 6 hours after 700 AM EST in a portion of northwest Georgia from Trenton to LaFayette to Calhoun and Cartersville to Rome and Summerville. Both heavy rain events caused widespread flash flooding and minor river flooding. As the instability increased during the day, the thunderstorms became more intense. Many of the thunderstorms were able to reach severe levels during the afternoon hours, causing primarily wind damage, with only isolated reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported a small creek overflowed a portion of McKown Road around 4 miles east to northeast of Douglasville. The culvert pipe was washed out with significant erosion damage to the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak mid-to-upper level shortwave swept across the Southern Appalachians early on the 2nd, with scattered showers and isolated thunderstorms developing early in the morning. During the day, the remnants of a squall line over the Lower Mississippi Valley pushed into the Southeast.  This combined with the shortwave and daytime heating allowed scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop.  Some thunderstorms reached severe levels with mainly damaging winds, but there were a couple of hail reports as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Wilkinson County Deputy Emergency Manager reported several trees down from near Clear Creek Baptist Church on Highway 18 to Dedrick Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of surface low pressure was in place from Alabama through the Ohio Valley, with a strong upper level system over the Missouri Valley.  The upper low retrograded to the west during the day on the 3rd while additional deep moisture pushed into the area from the Caribbean.  Showers and embedded thunderstorms developed during the afternoon, and with strong winds aloft associated with the upper low, some of the thunderstorms became strong, with one severe thunderstorm in central Georgia.  With saturated soils already in place, the additional rain on the 3rd led to additional flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of creeks were temporarily out of their banks and caused two roads to be closed due to flooding in a portion of central Haralson County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height at a USGS stream gage on the Big Creek at Atlanta Highway around 6 miles southwest of Cumming reached flood stage of 6 feet. The creek crest at 7.5 feet. Minor flooding occurred in the woodlands and walking paths of the The Big Creek Greenway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported several creeks and the upper reaches of the Toccoa River, especially in the Suches area were flooding numerous roads and threatening bridges. The Coosa Creek just west of Blairsville flooded residential yards and briefly entered a home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong upper high remained over the Plains and desert Southwest, with a weak upper low across the Bahamas and an upper trough over Canada.  A weak surface front was in place as well, stretching from the Great Lakes to the mid-Mississippi Valley.  A shortwave in the flow pushed southeast during the day, pushing the upper high to the west and the front to the south.  Once again, with ample moisture in place, showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon, lingering into the overnight hours.  Several thunderstorms produced flash flooding, and several became severe with trees blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Chattooga County Emergency Manager reported that trees were blown down from Menlo to Trion.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front slowly worked its way through the state on the 3rd, pushing into central Georgia during the late evening hours.  Enough instability developed during the afternoon for several thunderstorms to develop.  One thunderstorm reached severe levels in Macon County in central Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Middle Flint Regional E911 Center reported that structural damage occurred to a chicken house off of Garden Valley Road, and there was some roof damage to a mobile home in Ideal.  A few trees and some power lines were blown down and snapped off Plantation Road, and a few large trees were blown down blocking roads and causing power outages in Ideal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was located over southern Kentucky on the morning of the 19th, with a shortwave trough extending into northern Georgia. Tropical moisture prevailed over Georgia as well.  Widespread showers with embedded thunderstorms developed across a portion of north Georgia early in the morning and moved slowly south. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches occurred in less than 6 hours prior to 700 AM EST in an area from Dawsonville to Gainesville to Lawrenceville to Roswell. Significant flash flooding occurred with major damage to roads and bridges near Flowery Branch. Another 3 to 7 inches of rain occurred in 6 hours after 700 AM EST in a portion of northwest Georgia from Trenton to LaFayette to Calhoun and Cartersville to Rome and Summerville. Both heavy rain events caused widespread flash flooding and minor river flooding. As the instability increased during the day, the thunderstorms became more intense. Many of the thunderstorms were able to reach severe levels during the afternoon hours, causing primarily wind damage, with only isolated reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trudy Drive was completely washed out by a drainage branch flowing into Lake Lanier. There was a 50 to 75 foot large gap in the road with at least a 10 foot drop off. Several other roads were closed and damaged by flood waters as drainage branches become rapidly flowing creeks and overwhelmed culverts. These roads included portions of Atlanta Highway, McEver Road, Stephens Road, Flowery Way, Spring Street and Cove Creek Road in the Flowery Branch area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage on Dick Creek at Old Atlanta Road near Johns Creek and Suwanee reached flood stage of 10 feet. The creek crest at 12.8 feet. Minor flooding occurred with a few road closures nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Southeast remained in an active northwest flow pattern on the 1st, with an upper level disturbance moving through the area.  A weak surface trough remained in place across the state as well.  Showers and thunderstorms developed early in the morning as a result of the disturbance and tropical moisture already in place over the state.  A combination of back-building and training storms over the same areas caused intense rainfall amounts of 5 to 7 inches in less than 8 hours. This caused significant flash flooding in Pickens and Gilmer counties of north Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height of a USGS stream gage near Ellijay on the Coosawattee River off of Legion Road reached flood stage of 8 feet. The river crest at 9 feet which caused minor flooding of portions of Harold Hefner River Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was in place over the Southeast on the 21s, with an upper low over southeast Georgia and northeast Florida.  A very unstable airmass remained in place, and afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed across the state.  A couple were strong, and one was able to reach severe levels with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported nickel sized hail in Wrens.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved through the Mississippi Valley during the day and into the evening on the 22nd.  A shortwave moved across the Southeast, helping to focus shower and thunderstorm development.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed, and a couple of these were able to reach severe levels, bringing down trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported several trees down on Highway 278 east of Greensboro.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very moist airmass remained in place on the 1st, following heavy rain on June 30th that saturated soils.  A shortwave moved into the area from the west, adding weak synoptic lift to the moist and unstable July airmass.  A few thunderstorms were able to develop with isolated flash flooding reports.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height at a USGS stream gage on the Turnpike Creek at U.S. Highways 319 and 441 reached flood stage of 10 feet. The creek crest at 10.1 feet with minor flooding of woodlands and fields. The emergency manager reported a couple of road closures in the central portion of the county due to water covering roadways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of surface low pressure was in place from Alabama through the Ohio Valley, with a strong upper level system over the Missouri Valley. Showers and embedded thunderstorms developed during the afternoon, and with strong winds aloft associated with the upper low, some of the thunderstorms became strong, with one severe thunderstorm in central Georgia.  With saturated soils already in place, the additional rain on the 3rd led to additional flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported flood waters from a stream overflowed a portion of Colorado Trail SW with at least 6 inches of water. The flooding occurred 6 miles west of East Point.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active northwest flow regime remained in place on the 24th, with a strong upper disturbance moving through the Southeast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media and newspaper articles reported numerous trees and power lines down on the northeast side of Columbus, near the Lake Bottom area.  A 57-year-old woman was killed when a tree limb fell on her in her back yard at the 1200 block of 16th Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A departing upper shortwave established an active northwest flow regime across the Southeast on the 23rd.  A series of upper level disturbances moved through, helping to trigger showers and thunderstorms.  One thunderstorm became severe with downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Dodge County Emergency Manager reported that around a dozen trees were blown down from near Gresston to near Chauncey, with most of them down in the Eastman area.  Several power outages were reported as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active convective pattern continued on the 22nd as an upper trough over the Mississippi Valley pushed east during the day.  A strong disturbance pushed around the southern edge of the trough during the afternoon hours, and combined with the continued warm and moist airmass in place, triggered numerous showers and thunderstorms.  A couple of these thunderstorms became severe with downed trees, and one thunderstorm led to flash flooding overnight.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hall County Emergency Manager reported that a couple of trees were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pickens County emergency manager reported swift water rescues in progress at Sharptop Cove Young Life Camp off of Camp Hope Road northeast of Jasper. A portion of the road was washed out by Long Swamp Creek which was cutting off the camp. Two camp employees in a truck attempted to cross the flood waters and were washed downstream around 40 yards from the culvert. A portion of Tamarack Drive was completely washed out by the same creek around one quarter mile downstream from the camp. Another swift water rescue and evacuation from a home was in progress off of Price Creek Trail. Flood waters from Price Creek were threatening the home and first responders were having difficulty reaching the home due to a washed out road. A landslide around 100 feet wide and around 40 feet high closed one lane on a portion of Georgia Highway 136. In summary, 3 evacuations and 3 swift water rescues of people occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Middle Georgia EMC reported a few power outages across the county, mainly in the south and southwest portion, mostly caused by downed limbs.  The Wilcox County 911 Center reported that a couple of trees were blown down in Pitts as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough began to deepen over the Mississippi Valley on the 16th.  Several disturbances rotated around the base of the trough, triggering shower and thunderstorm development.  One of these thunderstorms produced isolated flash flooding in Houston County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County emergency manager reported flood waters from drainage ditches and small streams overflowed portions of around 10 roads in the Centerville area. Some minor flooding of a few residential yards near the streams occurred. The flood waters flowed into the Bay Gall Creek basin.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Crisp County Deputy emergency manager reported several small creeks and streams came out of their banks and flooded portions of some roads in eastern crisp county. The soils were wet from previous rainfall during the past 30 days.

The Chattahoochee River rose out of its banks and flooded the paddocks and access road to the stables at the Huntcliff River Club near Sandy Springs. Emergency officials evacuated 20 horses from the stables.

Multiple swift water rescues occurred with several evacuations of homes due to rapidly rising flood waters. Around 8 roads were either washed out or damaged...one bridge was washed out at Holt Road...around 25 structures were flooded with some washed off their foundations...8 people needed swift water rescues and 10 people were evacuated from structures. The Mountain Valley Farm Store and two large chicken houses off of Homer Wright Road were flooded and heavily damaged. It was estimated around 170,000 chickens were killed. The Georgia EPD condemned the area and had the chickens destroyed and properly buried.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A couple of roads were reported underwater in rural northern Irwin county.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports were received of high water along Highway 133 and in many other places across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Slow moving thunderstorms developed in a very moist, low shear airmass over northeast Georgia. A small area of flash flooding was reported in association with the storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A home was flooded by Toms Creek along Turner Road. The occupants had to be rescued. Highway 17 was reported to be flooded near Eastanollee, especially around Ogg���s Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was located over southern Kentucky on the morning of the 19th, with a shortwave trough extending into northern Georgia. Tropical moisture prevailed over Georgia as well.  Widespread showers with embedded thunderstorms developed across a portion of north Georgia early in the morning and moved slowly south. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches occurred in less than 6 hours prior to 700 AM EST in an area from Dawsonville to Gainesville to Lawrenceville to Roswell. Significant flash flooding occurred with major damage to roads and bridges near Flowery Branch. Another 3 to 7 inches of rain occurred in 6 hours after 700 AM EST in a portion of northwest Georgia from Trenton to LaFayette to Calhoun and Cartersville to Rome and Summerville. Both heavy rain events caused widespread flash flooding and minor river flooding. As the instability increased during the day, the thunderstorms became more intense. Many of the thunderstorms were able to reach severe levels during the afternoon hours, causing primarily wind damage, with only isolated reports of large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Upson County 911 Center reported several trees and power lines down across roads on the north side of Thomaston.  A few other trees were reported down in the eastern part of the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was in place over the Southeast on the 21s, with an upper low over southeast Georgia and northeast Florida.  A very unstable airmass remained in place, and afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed across the state.  A couple were strong, and one was able to reach severe levels with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported quarter sized hail in Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The front that approached the Southeast on the 10th remained stationary across north Georgia early on the 12th, but began to drift south during the day.  The axis of deep tropical moisture remained over portions of Georgia, and once again showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon.  Several of these became severe with mainly trees down, though there was one report of severe hail.  One thunderstorm also produced flash flooding in north Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Spalding County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down across Mount Zion Road at the intersection of Vaughn Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain caused very wet soil conditions across portions of central Georgia. Additional rainfall amounts around two inches in a few counties caused long-term areal flooding which washed out culverts or damaged several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Warren County emergency manager reported additional rainfall of around two inches on already wet soils caused the drainage systems to be overwhelmed leading to some washed out roads in the Warrenton area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain caused very wet soil conditions across portions of central Georgia. Additional rainfall amounts around two inches in a few counties caused long-term areal flooding which washed out culverts or damaged several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Johnson County emergency manager reported numerous washed out or flooded roads in the county and around Wrightsville. Additional rainfall of around two inches on already wet soils overwhelmed the drainage systems. In some areas there was so much water that maintenance crews could not get out to fix the box culverts and washed out roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A ridge of high pressure was in place from the Carolinas across to north Georgia early on the 22nd.  A very moist airmass was associated with this area of high pressure, and as a shortwave moved across the Southern Appalachians, several showers and thunderstorms developed.  A couple of these produced isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dawson County officials reported several drainage branches and streams were flooding portions of roads primarily north and southeast of Dawsonville. Some of the roads included Grizzle Road, Harry Sosebee Road, Tanner Hall Road, Blacks Mill Road and Nix Bridge Road. A house and car were damaged by flood waters in the 2700 block of Lumpkin Campground Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A trough of low pressure was positioned over the midlands while a very moist and unstable atmosphere was present over the southeastern United States.  Thunderstorms developed late afternoon as a mid level shortwave passed over the area. A few thunderstorms became strong as they encountered surface based cape values near 4000 J/KG and lifted index values between -8 to -9 C.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bulloch County 911 call center reported power lines down on campus.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of surface low pressure was in place from Alabama through the Ohio Valley, with a strong upper level system over the Missouri Valley.  The upper low retrograded to the west during the day on the 3rd while additional deep moisture pushed into the area from the Caribbean.  Showers and embedded thunderstorms developed during the afternoon, and with strong winds aloft associated with the upper low, some of the thunderstorms became strong, with one severe thunderstorm in central Georgia.  With saturated soils already in place, the additional rain on the 3rd led to additional flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported several trees down on Highway 36 along the Talbot/Upson County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper level trough remained in place over the center of the country, with a Bermuda high to the east, leaving the Southeast in a continued prolonged, moist tropical flow.  Showers and thunderstorms developed again in the afternoon hours, with one thunderstorm dropping enough rain to result in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The emergency manager reported several roads were washed out in and around Wrightsville. A couple of homes also had water in them.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of surface low pressure was in place from Alabama through the Ohio Valley, with a strong upper level system over the Missouri Valley. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same general pattern remained in place on the 6th that had been in place for the past several days, with a strong upper trough over the center of the country and a strong upper high over the western Atlantic, combining to continue the moist flow into the Southeast.  A line of showers and thunderstorms developed in eastern Alabama during the afternoon and moved into northwest Georgia, causing isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County emergency manager reported a stream flooded a portion of Daryl |Road near Silver Creek and was closed. The Little Cedar Creek came out of its banks in Cave Spring and caused minor flooding in Rolater Park.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The USGS stream gage on Pew Creek at Patterson Road near Lawrenceville reached flood stage of 11 feet. The creek crest at 14.3 feet. Moderate flooding occurred and the water level hit the bridge for around one hour. The USGS stream gage on Suwanee Creek at Buford Highway near Suwanee reached flood stage of 8 feet. The creek crest at 11.8 feet. Moderate flooding occurred with several road closures nearby. The USGS stream gage on Sweetwater Creek at Club Drive near Liburn reached flood stage of 11 feet. The creek crest at 13.1 feet. Minor flooding occurred. Long lake overflowed and flooded parts of the Riverbrooke neighborhood near Duluth. Backyards were flooded and the water came close to the houses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved through the Mississippi Valley during the day and into the evening on the 22nd.  A shortwave moved across the Southeast, helping to focus shower and thunderstorm development.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed, and a couple of these were able to reach severe levels, bringing down trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Bibb County 911 Center reported trees down along High Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The same general pattern remained in place on the 6th that had been in place for the past several days, with a strong upper trough over the center of the country and a strong upper high over the western Atlantic, combining to continue the moist flow into the Southeast. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Polk County officials reported significant flooding on Lake Creek. A portion of Lake Creek Road was flooded and closed due to overflowing water from the creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The upper low that had been across the center of the country finally pushed east and into the Mid-Atlantic on the 8th, shifting the deep moisture plume slightly to the south.  However, with a weak shortwave dropping into north Georgia during the afternoon, showers and thunderstorms developed again, resulting in isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hall County officials relayed a report of numerous roads under water in the Gainesville area. A few cars had stalled out in the flood waters covering the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The overall pattern had shifted by the 9th with an upper high over the Southern Plains, a weak trough over New England and the Mid-Atlantic, and a weakness over the Bahamas.  With the trough to the north, a shortwave was able to push into the Southeast during the afternoon.  The deep moisture plume continued in place, and so showers and thunderstorms developed again in the afternoon.  One thunderstorm led to isolated flash flooding, and another became severe with several trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Carroll County emergency manager reported the Buffalo Creek came out of its banks and flooded a portion of a road around 5 miles south of Carrollton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gwinnett Times reported that a tree was down on Interstate 85 near Buford Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low was located over southern Kentucky on the morning of the 19th, with a shortwave trough extending into northern Georgia. Tropical moisture prevailed over Georgia as well.  Widespread showers with embedded thunderstorms developed across a portion of north Georgia early in the morning and moved slowly south. Rainfall amounts of 3 to 7 inches occurred in less than 6 hours prior to 700 AM EST in an area from Dawsonville to Gainesville to Lawrenceville to Roswell. Significant flash flooding occurred with major damage to roads and bridges near Flowery Branch. Another 3 to 7 inches of rain occurred in 6 hours after 700 AM EST in a portion of northwest Georgia from Trenton to LaFayette to Calhoun and Cartersville to Rome and Summerville. EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported several small creeks came out of their banks and briefly flooded portions of nearby roads in the Cooper Heights area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Tropical Storm Karen were absorbed into a frontal system that pushed across Georgia between the 6th and 7th.  Plentiful moisture was in place from these two systems and widespread rain between 1 to 2 inches fell across the area, with isolated higher amounts.  One area across the northeast Atlanta Metro area received at least 3 inches of rain, which led to isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The stage height at a USGS stream gage on the Big Creek at Atlanta Highway 6 miles southwest of Cumming reached flood stage. The creek crest around 6 feet. Minor flooding occurred on large portions of the sidewalks and boardwalks of the Big Creek Greenway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong upper high remained over the Plains and desert Southwest, with a weak upper low across the Bahamas and an upper trough over Canada.  A weak surface front was in place as well, stretching from the Great Lakes to the mid-Mississippi Valley.  A shortwave in the flow pushed southeast during the day, pushing the upper high to the west and the front to the south.  Once again, with ample moisture in place, showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon, lingering into the overnight hours.  Several thunderstorms produced flash flooding, and several became severe with trees blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Polk County 911 Center reported that a tree was blown down in Antioch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Previous heavy rain and flash flooding on May 4th and 5th caused minor flooding on the Oostanaula River and its associated tributaries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Calhoun Times Newspaper reported a car drove into flood waters off Thomas Bluff Road just inside Floyd County after a high speed chase with the Gordon County Police.  The driver got out and tried to swim away.  The person drowned and his body was found four days later.

Etowah River Road was completely washed out where the dirt portion of it parallels the river. The road is expected to be closed for the next 2 weeks for repairs. A landslide occurred on a portion of Georgia Highway 53 near War Hill Park Road. Two feet of mud covered one lane with only one lane passable.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge was in place over the Southeast on the 21s, with an upper low over southeast Georgia and northeast Florida.  A very unstable airmass remained in place, and afternoon showers and thunderstorms developed across the state.  A couple were strong, and one was able to reach severe levels with large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported penny sized hail in Sandersville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong upper high remained over the Plains and desert Southwest, with a weak upper low across the Bahamas and an upper trough over Canada.  A weak surface front was in place as well, stretching from the Great Lakes to the mid-Mississippi Valley. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level high over over the western Atlantic began to shift west into Florida on the 11th, and a weak surface front remained stalled over Tennessee.  Once again, several showers and thunderstorms developed, and one produced flash flooding across White County, where flash flooding had occurred on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White County emergency manager reported the North Georgia Zoo near Camp Coleman Lake or around 6 miles west to northwest of Cleveland was flooded by Glade Creek. The animals were evacuated. Some damage to structures...bridges and fences occurred. Gravel or dirt roads and walkways were washed away.

Once again, several showers and thunderstorms developed, and one produced flash flooding across White County, where flash flooding had occurred on the 10th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The White County emergency manager reported Brasstown Creek came out of it banks and flooded a portion of Puddlebee Road around 6 miles northeast of Cleveland. A barn nearby was also flooded with animals stranded. The same creek further downstream flooded a portion of Roy Powers Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper trough moved through the Mississippi Valley during the day and into the evening on the 22nd.  A shortwave moved across the Southeast, helping to focus shower and thunderstorm development.  Scattered to numerous thunderstorms developed, and a couple of these were able to reach severe levels, bringing down trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed a report of a downed tree on Mock Springs Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain caused very wet soil conditions across portions of central Georgia. Additional rainfall amounts around two inches in a few counties caused long-term areal flooding which washed out culverts or damaged several roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Jefferson County emergency manager reported washed out roads near Avera. Almost two inches of rainfall in a 3 hour period on already wet soils quickly overwhelmed the drainage systems.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong southerly flow developed within the warm sector ahead of a potent cold front. Wind gusts reached as high as 40-50 mph across parts of the area due to strong gradient winds well in advance of the cold front. A line of low topped convection with little to no lightning entered western portions of the forecast area shortly after 8 pm and moved off the coast by midnight, quickly followed by the cold front itself. Strong dynamical forcing accompanied the front, but surface based instability was near zero most of the evening. The low topped convection was supported by 0-6 km shear values of 60-80 kt along with favorable upper level divergence from a 150 kt jet. Mid level lapse rates were poor throughout the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: HAM Radio operator relayed a report from the Long County Fire Department of a tree down on a car. No injuries reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave pushed through the Southeast on the 17th, with an axis of deep moisture in place across mainly north Georgia.  Once again, numerous showers and thunderstorms developed, and a few of these had enough rainfall to produce isolated flash flooding.  A couple of these thunderstorms became strong with isolated reports of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A farm pond in northwest Coweta County overflowed and briefly flooded a portion of Adcock Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 24th, a warm front was situated along the Georgia/Florida border, with an area of surface low pressure along the Florida Panhandle.  The surface low worked its way slowly east and northeast along the warm front as it lifted north into central Georgia during the morning hours.  A shortwave aloft pushed through the Southeast, helping provide lift for the convection that developed.  Several thunderstorms developed during the morning hours; some reached severe levels with mainly hail and some tree damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Banks County Emergency Manager reported that an old chicken house and a barn sustained minor damage from strong winds along Carson Segars Road.  Several small trees were blown down as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early on the 24th, a warm front was situated along the Georgia/Florida border, with an area of surface low pressure along the Florida Panhandle.  The surface low worked its way slowly east and northeast along the warm front as it lifted north into central Georgia during the morning hours.  A shortwave aloft pushed through the Southeast, helping provide lift for the convection that developed.  Several thunderstorms developed during the morning hours; some reached severe levels with mainly hail and some tree damage reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported that a tree was blown down along Durham Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper ridge remained over the Ohio Valley and Great Lakes area, with easterly flow dominating across the Southeast.  An upper disturbance moving across in the easterly flow combined with the continued moist and unstable airmass allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop during the afternoon and evening hours.  Many of these became severe with damaging winds and even some reports of large hail.  Isolated flash flooding was reported as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Oconee County Sheriff's Office reported several trees down east of Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed surface low moved through the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys on the 5th.  This pushed a cold front across Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours.  A squall line ahead of the front state, with damage occurring in northwest Georgia in the early afternoon and continuing into central Georgia through the mid-evening hours.  Widespread wind damage, mainly in the form of fallen trees, occurred, but one tornado was observed as well in Glascock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cherokee County Emergency Manager reported that a couple of trees were blown along Dr. John T Petit Street in Canton.  One tree fell on a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The strong upper high remained over the Plains and desert Southwest, with a weak upper low across the Bahamas and an upper trough over Canada.  A weak surface front was in place as well, stretching from the Great Lakes to the mid-Mississippi Valley.  A shortwave in the flow pushed southeast during the day, pushing the upper high to the west and the front to the south.  Once again, with ample moisture in place, showers and thunderstorms developed in the afternoon, lingering into the overnight hours.  Several thunderstorms produced flash flooding, and several became severe with trees blown down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Houston County emergency manager reported a portion of Barrett Road near Klondike had a culvert washed out which damaged the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 2 to 5 inches of rain in a couple of hours over portions of Columbia County resulting in flash flooding of several streams with water getting into homes and closing roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported 5 driveways washed out on Shallowford Place off of Columbia Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of strong storms developed across south central and southwest Georgia, producing isolated reports of trees down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A single tree was blown down near the intersection of U.S. Highway 84 and Old Naylor Road with no other damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed across southern Georgia and moved into northern Florida, producing occasional instances of damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down along Highway 39.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very large storm system approached the state on the 30th.  A highly amplified upper trough over the plains pushed east during the day, dragging a strong cold front through the Southeast.  A squall line associated with the cold front pushed into northwest and west central Georgia during the mid-morning hours, and pushed through the rest of the state through the afternoon and evening.  Widespread wind damage occurred with this line as it moved through.  Additionally, a supercell thunderstorm ahead of the line spawned two tornadoes; a rare January EF-3 across Bartow and Gordon Counties and an EF-1 across Gilmer and Fannin Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taylor County Emergency Manager reported that several trees were blown down across the county between 345 and 400 PM, especially along Highway 96 both west and east of Butler.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong closed surface low moved through the Ohio and Tennessee Valleys on the 5th.  This pushed a cold front across Georgia during the afternoon and evening hours.  A squall line ahead of the front state, with damage occurring in northwest Georgia in the early afternoon and continuing into central Georgia through the mid-evening hours.  Widespread wind damage, mainly in the form of fallen trees, occurred, but one tornado was observed as well in Glascock County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Floyd County Emergency Manager reported that a few small trees were blown down.  Pea-sized hail also occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wet pattern developed across the area during the middle of August with persistent rounds of showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe.  The heavy rainfall saturated grounds and led to instances of flooding and flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Highway 37 east of Highway 93 with water running over numerous other roads in the county with hazard signs posted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper low across the northern Gulf of Mexico allowed increasing moisture to filter into the Southeast on the 20th.  A strong shortwave moved out of the Gulf during the morning hours and across the Southeast during the afternoon.  Showers and thunderstorms with heavy rain developed again, leading to isolated flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Atlanta local media and Hall County emergency manager confirmed a portion of C. Rogers Road near White Sulpher in north Hall County was washed out due to a culvert pipe being overwhelmed by flood waters from localized intense rainfall.  The washed out road caused around 100 families to become isolated with no other alternative route.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms produced 2 to 5 inches of rain in a couple of hours over portions of Columbia County resulting in flash flooding of several streams with water getting into homes and closing roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: EM reported lightning struck a home and did moderate damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and mid level short wave shifted southeast over a warm and moist air in southeast Georgia. A few thunderstorms developed along the front and became severe with damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Liberty County 911 call center reported a tree down on a power line near the intersection of West Highway 196 and Joyner Road. The damage was likely caused by outflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep moisture plume continued across the Southeast, funneled between the upper high to the east and an upper low to the west.  Showers and thunderstorms developed again during the afternoon, leading to isolated flash flooding in northwest Georgia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Whitfield County emergency manager reported Bates Branch around 5 miles north of Dalton came out of its banks and flooded nearby residential yards. A family was evacuated from a home off Williams Road which was threatened by the flood waters. No damage occurred to the house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad area of surface low pressure was in place from Alabama through the Ohio Valley, with a strong upper level system over the Missouri Valley.  The upper low retrograded to the west during the day on the 3rd while additional deep moisture pushed into the area from the Caribbean.  Showers and embedded thunderstorms developed during the afternoon, and with strong winds aloft associated with the upper low, some of the thunderstorms became strong, with one severe thunderstorm in central Georgia.  With saturated soils already in place, the additional rain on the 3rd led to additional flash flooding across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters from several creeks in central Coweta County affected a few athletic areas including the soccer fields off of Georgia Highway 34. The White Oak Creek flooded portions of the Summergrove Golf Course and the water reached the garage of a home on the east side of Newnan.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Prevailing SW flow and high diurnal instability combined with a lingering frontal zone over SE Ga triggered severe storms across the area. Sea breeze and outflow enhanced storm development in the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees were blown down and damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A shortwave pushed through the Southeast on the 17th, with an axis of deep moisture in place across mainly north Georgia.  Once again, numerous showers and thunderstorms developed, and a few of these had enough rainfall to produce isolated flash flooding.  A couple of these thunderstorms became strong with isolated reports of downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Meriwether County Emergency Manager reported that two trees fell on houses in Luthersville, one on Gilbert Circle.  Five to six other trees were down across town as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another shortwave moved into the Southeast on the afternoon of the 24th.  Continued ample Gulf and Atlantic moisture was in place, and thus numerous showers and thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and evening hours.  There was enough instability in place for some of these to become severe with damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Laurens County Emergency Manager reported several trees down across the Dublin area, including some along the intersection of Highway 257 and Dublin-Eastman Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep layer shear, steep mid-level lapse rates, and sufficient instability came together to produce several severe thunderstorms across the area.  Large hail was the primary threat, although some isolated occurrences of wind damage also occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Golf ball size hail was confirmed via a photograph about 5 miles east of Adel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid level impulse tracked southward from SE Georgia to over north Florida during the afternoon. This feature combined with the west coast sea breeze and outflows interacted over a moist and unstable airmass which spawned scattered severe storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down near Henry Rowell Road just west of Nicholls. There were also blown down in Nicholls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of passing upper level short wave energy...lingering low level outflow boundaries from prior convective activity...and the sea breezes spawned scattered severe storms during the afternoon and early evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread tree damage occurred across the county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture combined with a weakening cold front front approaching southwest Georgia in concert with the east coast sea breeze and outflow boundaries...sparked scattered strong and rotating storms over the area during the afternoon and evening near the east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported just east of the St. Simons Island Airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak cold front lingered over south Georgia while a moist tropical airmass streamed over the area under southwest flow. Locally heavy rainfall was expected due to slow storm motion...and a few strong storms were expected during the afternoon due to outflows from morning convection over western zones interacting with an unstable low level airmass.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm spotter reported minor street flooding in Waycross, mainly in the vicinity of the fairgrounds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Tropical Storm Andrea crossed the forecast area from the Gulf of Mexico. SE Ga and NE Fl were on the northeast side of the storm...which focused onshore convergent bands. A few tornadoes were spawned along the Duval and Nassau county Atlantic coasts...with gusty tropical storm force winds and locally heavy rainfall over inland areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia Highway Patrol reported a funnel cloud near Interstate 95.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture combined with a weakening cold front front approaching southwest Georgia in concert with the east coast sea breeze and outflow boundaries...sparked scattered strong and rotating storms over the area during the afternoon and evening near the east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was spotted near Interstate 95 north of Brunswick near the Golden Isles Parkway. The time given was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture combined with a weakening cold front front approaching southwest Georgia in concert with the east coast sea breeze and outflow boundaries...sparked scattered strong and rotating storms over the area during the afternoon and evening near the east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia State Patrol received a funnel cloud report near Brunswick. The time was based on radar.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture combined with a weakening cold front front approaching southwest Georgia in concert with the east coast sea breeze and outflow boundaries...sparked scattered strong and rotating storms over the area during the afternoon and evening near the east coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Georgia State Patrol received a funnel cloud report on State Road 25 northwest of Jekyll Island. The time was based on radar.
